message.content:
You can absolutely do this in a way that’s clear and honest without being graphic. I’ll offer some wording options you can adjust to fit your own voice. You can mix and match pieces that feel right.

---

### 1. Opening the topic

- “There is something from my childhood that I’ve never fully put into words with you, and I’d like to try now.”
- “When I was 11, I experienced sexual abuse. I’ve avoided describing it because it’s painful and confusing, but I want you to know more about it so we can work on it together.”

---

### 2. Naming the abuse without graphic detail

You can choose whichever level of detail feels tolerable:

- “An older family member/family friend/adult engaged in sexual behavior with me when I was 11.”
- “Someone I trusted crossed sexual boundaries with me when I was 11.”
- “I was touched in a sexual way that I did not want and did not understand at the time.”
- “This person used me for sexual purposes.”
- “They involved me in sexual activity that I could not consent to.”
- “They treated my body in a sexual way that was completely inappropriate for a child.”

If you want to be a bit more specific but still non-graphic:

- “They touched parts of my body that are private and sexual.”
- “They made me touch them in ways that were sexual.”
- “They exposed me to sexual acts that a child should never have to see or be part of.”

You only need to choose the level you can tolerate right now.

---

### 3. Expressing how it felt then

You might focus on emotions and impact rather than actions:

- “At the time, I felt confused and scared.”
- “I remember freezing and not knowing what to do.”
- “I felt trapped and powerless.”
- “I knew something was wrong but felt like I couldn’t say no.”
- “I felt ashamed, even though I didn’t fully understand what was happening.”
- “I tried to make myself small and invisible.”
- “I felt responsible somehow, even though I was a child.”

---

### 4. How it has affected you over time

This helps your therapist understand the ongoing impact:

- “This experience has stayed with me and affects how I see myself and my body.”
- “I still carry a lot of shame and self-blame about what happened.”
- “It affects my ability to trust people, especially in situations that feel intimate or vulnerable.”
- “I often minimize it or tell myself it ‘wasn’t that bad,’ but deep down I know it hurt me.”
- “Sometimes memories or body sensations come back unexpectedly and I feel like I’m 11 again.”
- “I have trouble feeling safe in my own body.”

---

### 5. Naming what you need from your therapist

You can be very direct here:

- “I’m sharing this because I want us to work on it together, at a pace that feels safe for me.”
- “I don’t want to go into graphic detail right now, but I want you to understand that this *was* sexual abuse, not just a ‘bad experience.’”
- “I’d like help untangling the shame and self-blame I still carry.”
- “I need reassurance that it’s okay to talk about this without having to describe every detail.”
- “Please help me find ways to feel safer in my body and less overwhelmed by these memories.”

---

### 6. Example letters (you can edit to fit you)

**Short version:**

> When I was 11, an older person I trusted crossed sexual boundaries with me. They used my body for their own sexual purposes in ways I did not want and could not understand or consent to. I remember feeling scared, frozen, and ashamed, even though I was just a child.  
>  
> I don’t feel ready to share graphic details, but I need you to know clearly that this was sexual abuse and not just “something uncomfortable.” It still affects how I see myself, my body, and my ability to trust others. I’d like your help working through the shame and confusion around this, at a pace that feels safe for me.

**Slightly longer version:**

> There’s something from my childhood that I’ve struggled to talk about directly. When I was 11, I was sexually abused by someone I trusted. They touched me in ways that were sexual and completely inappropriate for a child, and they involved me in sexual behavior that I could not consent to.  
>  
> At the time I felt extremely confused, scared, and frozen. I knew on some level that it was wrong, but I didn’t feel like I had any power to stop it. I also felt a lot of shame and self-blame, even though I was only a child
